publisher.publisher-name: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

title-group.article-title: Prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of Cryptosporidium spp. infection among calves in the Far-North Region of Cameroon

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Parasitology and Parasitic Pathology, School of Sciences and Veterinary Medicine, University of Ngaoundere, P.O. Box 454 Ngaoundere, Cameroon.
2Department of Epidemiology and Public Health, Pasteur Center of Cameroon, P.O. Box 1274 Yaounde, Cameroon.
3Special Mission of Tse-Tse flies Eradication, P.O. Box 263 Ngaoundere, Cameroon.
4Department of Biological Sciences, Faculty of Science, University of Ngaoundere, P.O. Box 454 Ngaoundere, Cameroon.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Augustin Siama. Department of Parasitology and Parasitic Pathology, School of Sciences and Veterinary Medicine, University of Ngaoundere, P.O. Box 454 Ngaoundere, Cameroon. siamaaugustin@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Korean Society of Veterinary Science
permissions.copyright-holder: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Importance
sec.p: Cryptosporidiosis is a ubiquitous gastrointestinal zoonotic disease of young calves and children under 5 years of age, but it is relatively unknown in Cameroon.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study examined the prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of cryptosporidiosis in calves of the Far North Region of Cameroon.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Fecal samples were collected and examined using the modified Ziehl-Neelsen staining technique for Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 261/629 calves tested positive for infection, giving an overall 41.5% prevalence. The prevalence was significantly higher in calves younger than 3 months (59.68%) (p < 0.000), with a body condition score (BCS) 2 (69.56%) (p<0.000), with diarrheic stools (74.72%), during the rainy season (74.72%) (p < 0.000), and in large herds (48.16%) (p = 0.046). This disease was distributed widely in all divisions and localities of the study. The risk of infection was higher in calves aged < 3 months (p < 0.000), with diarrheic stools (p < 0.000), BCS 2 (p < 0.000), during the rainy season (p < 0.000), and in large herds (p < 0.000). The prevalence of cryptosporidiosis was distributed widely in diarrhoeic calves (p < 0.000).

sec.title: Conclusions and Relevance
sec.p: This study provides the first evidence of Cryptosporidium spp. infection of calves in the Far North region of Cameroon with the associated risk factors such as age, BCS, season, herd size, diarrheal status, feed source, barn hygiene, and water sources. Further molecular investigation in this region will be needed to implement control strategies to limit zoonotic transmission to humans.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Cryptosporidiosis is a parasitic disease caused by a protozoan of the genus Cryptosporidium, which infects the epithelial cells of the digestive tracts of numerous animals, including humans [12]. The veterinary significance of cryptosporidiosis stems from the diarrheal disorders in young animals [23]. This disease can be zoonotic, linked to Cryptosporidium parvum, and is most pathogenic in children under 5 years of age and immunocompromised individuals [2]. Infection by the parasite causes mild diarrhea in immunocompetent patients and severe diarrhea (potentially fatal) in immunocompromised patients, such as those suffering from acquired immunodeficiency syndrome [3]. Young calves, children, and immunocompromised individuals are frequently diagnosed with the disease. The parasite of the genus Cryptosporidium has a complex life cycle, including both sexual and asexual reproduction, an auto-infectious cycle for immunocompromised individuals, and the ability to complete its development within a single host [4]. The contamination of water and food sources by oocysts from infected mammals and wastewater can lead to epidemics in animals and humans. Many species of animals can be infected by cryptosporidiosis oocysts because of their ability to withstand harsh environmental conditions and water treatment processes [14].

In Cameroon, there is limited knowledge about the epidemiology of the disease in calves nationwide, particularly in the Far North Region. The only trustworthy evidence of cryptosporidiosis was reported by Tombang et al. [5], who revealed a 16.97% prevalence among children aged 0–5 years in Limbe, South-West Region. The farming system is still conventional in this vital livestock-raising zone of the country, with unsanitary rearing practices believed to promote oocyst contamination of calves. Moreover, most public health professionals and veterinarians are unaware of the disease and tend to associate it with other common diarrheal conditions or gastrointestinal helminthiasis, leading to significant losses. This study examined the epidemiology and impact of cryptosporidiosis in calves in the Far North Region.

sec.title: Study area and sites
sec.p: The study took place in the Far North Region of Cameroon from August 2022 to July 2023. This region has an area of 35.264 km2 and lies between latitudes 10° and 13° N and longitudes 13° and 15° E. The region is bordered to the south by the northern region, to the east by the Republic of Chad, and to the west by the Federal Republic of Nigeria [6]. Its estimated population of 5.5 million is comprised of several ethnic groups, including Guiziga, Mufu, Mandara, Kapsiki, Mafa, Musgum, Massa, Mufou, Muktélé, Podoko, Mada, Zulgo, Fulani, Mundang, and Tupuri [6]. The climate is tropical, of the Sudan–Sahelian type, with a short rainy season of 3 to 4 months (June to September) and a long dry season of 8 to 9 months (October to May). The average annual rainfall is between 750 and 800 mm, ranging from 500 to 900 mm. Between March and May, the highest temperatures vary between 37°C–40°C, while the lowest temperatures are between 16°C–18°C from December to February. The soil is sandy clay, hydromorphic, and ferruginous. Plants are dominated by herbaceous savannahs and steppes in vegetation [6].

The study was conducted on farms within Diamaré, Mayo-Tsanaga, Mayo-Kani, and Mayo-Danay Division (Fig. 1). These sites were selected based on their proximity, accessibility, and the availability of farmers and calves.

caption.title: Map presenting distribution of prevalence of infection by locality and division.
caption.p: Prevalence (%) represents prevalence of calves cryptosporidiosis; 34-35 represents prevalence of calf cryptosporidiosis between 34% and 35%; 36-43 represents prevalence of calf cryptosporidiosis between 36% and 43%; 44-45 represents prevalence of calf cryptosporidiosis between 44% to 55%.

sec.p: Fecal samples were taken from male and female calves in the herd that were less than 1-year-old. The Thrusfield [7] formula was used to calculate the minimum sample sizes with a significant threshold of 95% and a required precision of 5%. A 50% prevalence was assumed based on the rule of thumb. A simple random sampling of cattle was carried out using the following formula:
where n represents sample size; P is estimated prevalence; Zα is confidence level according to the reduced centred normal distribution (1.96); and α is tolerated margin of error (0.05).

The minimum sample size was calculated to be 384 calves. The sex was established by examining the anatomical features of the external genitalia. The age of the animals was determined by examining their dentition and gathering information from their owners or breeders regarding their date of birth. The breed was determined based on the phenotypic characteristics of the calves, with reference to their sire. Body condition scores (BCSs) ranging from zero to five were assigned based on the estimated overweight and classified according to scores of 2, 3, and 4 assigned for those in the “lean,” “good,” and “very good” categories, respectively.

sec.title: Collection and storage of samples
sec.p: The feces samples were collected from the calves either immediately after they were spontaneously emitted or after stimulating them with a gloved hand. At the time of collection, the date of collection, age of the calf, the sex, BCS, rearing system, consistency of the feces (diarrheal or not), herd size, and locality for each animal were noted on the collection tube and the survey form. The feces samples were preserved by adding a few drops of 10% neutral buffered formalin to the tubes immediately after sampling. The tubes were placed in a cooler, taken to the National Veterinary Laboratory in Garoua, and stored in a cold room at −4°C.

sec.title: Finding and identification of oocysts
sec.p: The oocysts were identified using the Ziehl–Nielsen staining procedure [8]. Indeed, the slides were allowed to air dry after 0.2 g of each fecal sample was extracted, emulsified using the vortex, and smeared over the slide using the loop that had been heated beforehand to create a thin smear. Subsequently, the slides were placed on the staining support, flooded with alcohol at 95°C for 5 min, and then flamed. They were immediately covered with warm phenol fuchsin for 5 min and rinsed with distilled water. A few drops of 95% HCl were applied to decolorize them and promptly rinsed. After 30 sec of counterstaining with methylene blue, the slides were rinsed again with distilled water, drained, and allowed to air dry for 10 min. After adding a drop of immersion oil to each slide, the slides were viewed under an optical microscope set to objectives ×40 and ×100. The oocysts of cryptosporidium were identified by their red color and form on a blue background [8].

sec.title: Calf management on farms
sec.p: After birth, the calves on each of these farms remained with their mothers for 2 weeks before being segregated and either tethered separately or kept in communal enclosures. Instead of grazing with their parents, they wandered the campgrounds. They drank from troughs next to water wells during the rainy season, as well as from natural water courses and bodies of water. The animal standing on its side was used to measure the cleanliness of the farms [3]. The places evaluated were below a line that ran from the tail head to the top of the shoulder. The following three classes, ranging from one to three, were used to rate the cleanliness of the grid: 1) Clean or small dirty, if there was no dirt on the animal or traces of dirt or if the areas of dirt extended over the lower half of the thigh and the lower part of the belly and sternum; 2) Dirty, if the areas of dirt extended from the top of the thigh (trochanter) to the front of the sternum; and 3) Very dirty, if the dirt extended from the buttock (hip) to the point of the shoulder. The dirt ran up the side to the top of the flank and formed a thick crust.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The prevalence and different risk factors were evaluated using a χ2 test on the acquired data. The prevalences of more than two variables were compared, and their significance was assessed using a Schwartz test (Z). The risk of the modalities within a single significant component was determined using logistic regression. A p value < 0.05 was considered significant.

sec.title: Prevalence and distribution of cryptosporidiosis
sec.p: Cryptosporidium oocysts (Fig. 2) were found in 261 of the 629 calves analyzed, i.e., 41.5% of calves had a cryptosporidiosis infection.

caption.title: Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts observed under microscope open arrow (objective ×100).

sec.title: Distribution according to sex
sec.p: The prevalence of cryptosporidiosis according to sex was 38.74% and 44% for male and female calves, respectively, but the difference was not significant (χ2 = 1.42; degree of freedom [df] = 1; p = 0.178).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis according to age
sec.p: The distribution of calf infections according to age (Table 1) revealed a prevalence of infection ranging from 12.35% in calves aged > 6 months to 59.68% in calves aged less than 3 months (χ2 = 71.39; df = 2; p = 0.000). The Z-test showed that calves under 3 months followed by 3–6 months were more infected (Z1 = 7.39; df = 2; p = 0.000) than the calves over 6 months (Z2 = 2.39; df = 2; p = 0.000).

caption.title: Univariable analyses of prevalence, distribution, and impact of calves’ cryptosporidiosis

table-wrap-foot.p: No., number; CI, confidence interval; BCS, body condition score.
a-cValues followed by the same letters are not significantly different at the 5% level.

sec.title: Distribution of infection according to season
sec.p: Table 1 lists the distribution of cryptosporidiosis prevalence according to the season. Calves were significantly more infested in the rainy season (48.42%) than in the dry season (32.86%) (χ2 = 15.50; df = 1; p = 0.000).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis as a function of herd size
sec.p: Table 1 lists the distribution of the prevalence of Cryptosporidium spp. infection as a function of the herd size. This prevalence was 37.36% and 48.16% for small and large herds, respectively (χ2 = 6.16; df = 2; p = 0.046). The Z test showed that the incidence of infection was significantly higher in large herds (Z1 = 7.39; df = 2; p = 0.05).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis according to feed sources
sec.p: Table 1 lists the prevalence of cryptosporidiosis according to the feed source of calves. Calves fed milk and pasture grasses were significantly more infected (43.89%) than those fed only pasture grasses (20.31%) (χ2 = 6.30; df = 1; p = 0.05).

sec.title: Distribution of infection according to hygienic conditions of the farm
sec.p: Table 1 lists the prevalence of cryptosporidiosis in relation to agricultural hygiene. Infection was more prevalent in calves from extremely dirty farms (50.32%), followed by those from dirty farms (35.94%), but lower in those from clean or small dirty farms (21.54%) (χ2 = 10.38; df = 1; p = 0.05).

sec.title: Distribution of infection according to the water source
sec.p: Table 1 lists the prevalence of cryptosporidiosis related to the source water. Calves that drank from natural water sources and boreholes showed significantly greater prevalences (5.76% and 42.3%, respectively) than those that drank just from boreholes (18.06%) (χ2 = 9.17; df = 1; p = 0.05).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis on occurrence of diarrhea
sec.p: Table 1 shows the prevalence of cryptosporidiosis according to whether the stool was diarrhoeic or not. Calves with diarrheal stools were substantially more infected (74.72%) than those with non-diarrheal stools (35.87%) (χ2 = 46.97; df = 1; p = 0.000).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis on BCS
sec.p: Table 1 lists the distribution of calf infections based on the BCS. The prevalence ranged considerably from 30.49% in calves with a BCS 4 to 69.56% in those with a BCS 2 (χ2 = 37.99; df = 3; p = 0.000). The Z test showed that calves with BCS 2, or lean, are the most infected (Z1 = 7.39; df = 2; p = 0.000). Therefore, cryptosporidiosis leads to weight loss and a lower BCS.

sec.title: Distribution of calf cryptosporidiosis according to the breed of calves
sec.p: The prevalence of infection ranged from 37.14% in calves of the Kapsiki breed to 48.71% in calves of the Massa breed, but this difference was not significant (χ2 = 2.3; df = 5; p = 0.80).

sec.title: Distribution of calves’ cryptosporidiosis according to the Division
sec.p: The map in Fig. 1 shows the prevalence of the disease in the division of Far North Region. This disease was found in all divisions, with a higher frequency in Mayo–Danay (44.23%), Diamare (44.09%), and Mayo–Kani (43.04%) and a lower prevalence in Mayo–Tsanaga (34.78%) but the difference was not significant (χ2 = 2.3; df = 5; p = 0.80).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis infections according to locality
sec.p: A cryptosporidiosis infection is distributed among calves in all the localities investigated (Fig. 1), with the highest prevalence in Yagoua (51.16%), followed by Bogo (45.88%), Kaele (45.55%), and Gazawa (42.10%). In contrast, the prevalence is slightly lower in Moutourwa, Mogode, Pouss, and Hina (39.34%, 37.75%, 37.71%, and 35.80%, respectively), but these differences are not statistically significant (χ2 = 2.3; df = 7; p = 0.80).

sec.title: Distribution of cryptosporidiosis according to the rearing system
sec.p: A study of the distribution of cryptosporidiosis according to the rearing system used on farms in the Far North Region varied from 41.34% and 41.56% prevalence in the calves of semi-extensive farming systems and extensive farming systems, respectively, but the difference was not significant (χ2 = 0.03; df = 1; p = 0.95).

sec.title: Risk factors for cryptosporidiosis
sec.p: Table 2 lists the logical regression of possible risk factors linked to cryptosporidiosis, indicating that calves younger than 3 months are 8.5 times more vulnerable than those older than 3 months (odds ratio [OR], 8.5; df = 2; p = 0.000). The risk was 4.7 times higher for BCS 2 calves than for BCS 3 and 4 calves (OR, 4.7; df = 2; p = 0.000). The odds of calves contracting the disease were 4.2 times higher for those with diarrheal feces than for those with regular stools (OR, 4.2; df = 1; p = 0.000). During the rainy season, calves had a 2.03-fold higher risk of infection than during the dry season (OR, 2.03; df = 1; p = 0.000). Large herds had a 1.3-fold higher risk of infection than medium and small herds (OR, 1.55; df = 2; p = 0.02). Calves that drank milk and grass from pasture were 1.23 times more likely to contract Cryptosporidium spp. Oocyst infections than those that just grazed on pasture (OR, 1.23; df = 1; p = 0.02). In terms of hygiene, calves from ordinary and bad hygiene farms were 2.34 and 4.98 times more likely to contract an infection, respectively, than calves from farms with acceptable hygiene (OR, 2.34; df = 2; p = 0.004 and OR, 4.98; df = 2; p = 0.004, respectively). Animals that drank water from natural watercourses or from both borehole and natural watercourses had a 2.3 and 2.01 times higher risk of contracting cryptosporidiosis, respectively, than those that only drank water from boreholes (OR, 2.3; df = 2; p = 0.03 and OR, 2.01; df = 2; p = 0.03, respectively).

caption.title: Logistic regression of the cryptosporidiosis risk factors

table-wrap-foot.p: OR, odds ratio; CI, confidence interval; BCS, body condition score.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The overall prevalence of cryptosporidiosis in calves under 1-year-old from the Far North region was 41.5% throughout this investigation. This prevalence was less than that of the studies conducted in Canada [9], England (45%) [10], and Japan (75%) [11]. Fayer et al. [12] in the USA (12%), Duranti et al. [13] in Italy (8%), Thompson et al. [1] in Northern Ireland (37%), Maikai et al. [14] in Nigeria (16%), and Berhanu et al. [15] in Ethiopia (20.5%) all recorded lower rates. Environmental variables and animal husbandry may be responsible for this discrepancy because they can either raise or lower the rate of sporulated oocysts based on the region.

Calves younger than 3 months and those between three and 6 months had higher infection rates (59.68% and 34.49%, respectively) than those older than 6 months (12.35%) (see infection distribution according to age). These findings concur with Yemane’s [16] findings from Ethiopia in those calves younger than 6 months had higher infestation rates than animals older than 6 months. Hussin et al. [17] also reported a high infection rate of 40% in Iraqi calves between the ages of one week and 3 months. By contrast, Dankwa et al. [18] in Ghana and Wade et al. [19] in New York revealed greater infection rates in calves older than 6 months. Owing to the immaturity of their immune systems, calves between the ages of 3 and 4 weeks are particularly vulnerable to contracting cryptosporidiosis, according to Desaultes [20]. Colostrum from contact with an oocyst-carrying mother contaminates the calf. The transmission of oocysts in pastures, stables, and watering holes, as well as contact with adults that are asymptomatic carriers (reservoirs), can account for the increase in infestation incidence with age [1820].

The wet season has a higher prevalence of cryptosporidiosis (48.42%) than the dry season (32.86%). According to Ola-Fadunsin et al. [21], the prevalence was higher in Nigeria during the rainy season (34.52%) than in the dry season (22.79%). Featherston et al. [14] also documented a higher incidence during the rainy season in England. Featherston et al. [10] and Ikiroma and Pollock [22] reported that the season has a significant impact on oocyst dissemination because the climate of the rainy season is favorable for oocyst dispersal, survival, and sporulation. On the other hand, high temperatures inhibit the propagation of oocysts during the dry season [22].

Calves living in large herds have a higher prevalence of Cryptosporidium spp. infections than those in medium and small herds, which is consistent with Garber et al. [23] in the US. According to Das et al. [24], herd size affects the presence of the parasite on farms because more animals mean more oocysts are expelled, and more animals are contaminated.

Calves with diarrheal illness shed oocysts more frequently than those with normal fecal matter, indicating a substantial correlation between the frequency of cryptosporidiosis and fecal consistency in calves. Das et al. [24] and Ehsan et al. [25] reported that animals with diarrheal stools also had higher infection rates than those without. By contrast, Ayinmode and Fagbemi [26] and Dankwa et al. [18] reported opposing results using the molecular technique; the results obtained validated the clinical aspect of the disease that causes diarrhea in patients. The development of diarrhea is believed to be related to the action of C. parvum oocysts, which attach to and kill intestinal microvilli and epithelia, reducing the absorptive activity and resulting in diarrhea [26]. Furthermore, the parasite may decrease the disaccharidase activity, which hinders the breakdown of sugars and causes bacterial growth, volatile fatty acid production, and osmotic pressure changes, all of which contribute to typical severe, watery diarrhea [12627].

In terms of the disease distribution linked to BCS, calves with BCS 2 had a considerably higher prevalence (69.56%) than calves with BCS 3 (38.88%) and BCS 4 (30.49%). Berhanu et al. [15], Yemane [16], and Birhanu et al. [27] reported similar findings in Ethiopia (30.4% in low BCS calves vs. 20.2% and 8.0% in average and good BCS calves, respectively). This high frequency in animals with BCS 2 might be related to the weight loss induced by prolonged cryptosporidiosis-induced diarrhea. In addition to yellowish, foul-smelling diarrhea, calves with clinical cryptosporidiosis frequently exhibit acidosis, lethargy, and dehydration. The appetite of the calf usually decreases if the diarrhea continues, which can result in weight loss and impaired growth [328].

The distribution associated with the feed source showing that calves fed both milk and pasture were more infected than those fed only pasture is in accordance with Ayele et al. [29], who reported a higher infection rate (23.4%) in feeding on milk and pasture than pasture alone (11.9%) in Ethiopia. These findings may be related to the Cryptosporidium oocyst contamination of colostrum, hay, water, and the stable, which increases the likelihood of contaminating young calves [2030]. El-Khodery and Osman [31] and Castro-Hermida et al. [32] suggested that a dirty and muddy farm might produce favorable microclimatic elements or conditions for the presence and survival of Cryptosporidium oocysts in farms or animal buildings, increasing the risk of food contamination and water.

According to the distribution of prevalence linked to farm hygienic conditions, calves were considerably more infected from farms with poor hygienic circumstances, followed by farms with average hygienic qualities, while the frequency of cryptosporidiosis was lower from farms with good hygienic conditions. Ayele et al. [29] also reported a strong correlation between the level of hygiene on the farm and Cryptosporidium infection. According to El-Khodery and Osman [31] and Castro-Hermida et al. [32], animals not properly cleaned were more likely to become infected and disseminate Cryptosporidium oocysts. Hence, a muddy and unclean farm may enhance feed and provide favorable microclimatic conditions for the growth or survival of Cryptosporidium oocysts in farms or animal buildings.

The higher prevalence in calves that drank from natural water sources and from both natural water sources and boreholes than calves that drank only from boreholes in the present study is similar to Ayele et al. [29] in Ethiopia and El-Khodery and Osman [31], who reported that river water could significantly affect the occurrence of Cryptosporidium infection in calves. This might be due to the contamination of wells/rivers that drain or run off from farming areas [2931]. According to El-Khodery and Osman [31] and Castro-Hermida et al. [32], a dirty and muddy farm can create favorable microclimatic factors or conditions for the presence and survival of Cryptosporidium oocysts in farms or livestock buildings, increasing the risk of contamination of food and water. On the other hand, the dumping of animal waste in the open air, human defecation, animal waste from adult cattle in the open air, and the survival of oocysts in water could explain these observations. Indeed, a single asymptomatic adult bovine can excrete between 9 × 102 and 1.8 × 104 oocysts per gram of feces, or 3.6 × 106 to 7.2 × 108 oocysts per animal per day, and allow oocysts to be disseminated, particularly when they are discharged into water [3334].

Age, BCS, season, herd size, diarrheal stool status, feed sources, farm hygiene conditions, and water sources were the risk factors linked to cryptosporidiosis in calves in this study. Morin [35] and Abderrazek et al. [36] reported similar findings. Nevertheless, other factors, such as resistance status, maternity, calf housing, environment, and calving season, were not considered in this study [2934]. Calves younger than 3 months are 8.5 times more vulnerable than those older than 3 months because the disease mostly affects calves that are more likely to contract it by udder suckling or contact with contaminated bedding [34]. Newborn calves may contract the disease early if their surroundings are unhealthy [37]. According to Morin [35], calf malnutrition or undernourishment, concurrent infections, stress, and the health of the mothers also have an adverse effect on the animal’s weight and the newborn calf’s resistance to the disease. Therefore, calves with BCS 2 are 4.7 times more exposed than those with BCS 3 and 4. The observations that calves with diarrheal stools are 4.2 times more likely to develop the disease than those with non-diarrheal stools are similar to those reported by Ehsan et al. [25] in Belgium, where animals with diarrheal stools were more at risk with an OR of 6.1. Factors such as dystocia, sex, twinning, and prematurity (giving birth to weak and fragile calves) can alter the resistance status of newborn calves and favor the appearance and severity of C. parvum diarrhea [38]. During the rainy season, oocyst dissemination, survival, and sporulation are facilitated by favorable climatic circumstances, which increases the likelihood of calves contracting the disease by 2.03-fold compared to the dry season [233139]. Overcrowding of animals, especially calves, is a key risk factor for the transmission of the parasite because each individual is a possible source of infection that might contaminate others, making large herds 1.3 times more likely to be infected than medium-sized and small herds [373839]. The probability of calves contracting Cryptosporidium oocyst infections and the prevalence of the disease increase with herd size, as stated by Morin [35] and Hussin et al. [17]. The results showing that calves from farms with poor and medium hygiene had a higher infection risk (4.98 and 2.34, respectively) than those from farms with better hygiene are comparable to those of Abderrazek et al. [36], who reported a link between infection and poor hygiene in the eastern part of Algeria. This outcome can be explained by the presence and buildup of abandoned feces, which can cause infectious oocysts to contaminate hay and water [2940].

Cryptosporidium infections are common in the Far North and pose a serious risk of zoonotic transmission to human populations, especially to the young and those with weakened immune systems. Therefore, more research on the molecular prevalence of the disease is necessary to determine its zoonotic impact and human transmission risk.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: The authors appreciate the Chief Laboratory Technologist of the Department of Parasitology of the National Veterinary Laboratory of Garoua in Cameroon, Mr. MEKE Christian, for her support on the examination of parasites.

p.italic: This paper was published with special support from the Korean Society of Veterinary Science.

fn.p: Conflict of Interest: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
Data Availability Statement: Data for this study are available upon request to the corresponding author.

list-item.p: Conceptualization: Siama A, Njan Nlôga AM.

Writing – review & editing: Siama A, Kalmobe J.
